CHAPTER 17
THE MODERN COMMONWEALTH
By
The Rt. Hon. DUNCAN SANDYS, MP
Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations
FOR reasons of history, geography, and economics, each member country naturally sees the Commonwealth from a somewhat different angle. I will try and explain what it means to us in Britain.
EMPIRE INTO COMMONWEALTH

The Commonwealth is a product of history. This unique association of independent member states has grown, step by step, out of the old British Empire. The process of evolution from colonial status to national sovereignty has been going on for a very long time. Responsible government was first instituted in Canada, Australia and New Zealand over a hundred years ago. After the first World War they became separate members of the League of Nations, and their sovereign independence was finally confirmed by the Statute of Westminster of 1931. In the years which followed it became clear that the constitutional evolution of Britain's other overseas territories would inevitably follow along the same road.

The most radical change of all took place in 1947 and 1948, when India, Pakistan and Ceylon attained full nationhood. Quite suddenly the independent Commonwealth was transformed from an association of about 70 million people of predominantly European stock into a multi-racial association, predominantly Asian, several hundred million strong. Since then Ghana, Malaya, Nigeria, Cyprus, Sierra Leone and Tanganyika have likewise become sovereign states within the Commonwealth.
THE FINAL STAGE

The independent nations of the Commonwealth now have a combined population of over 700 million, nearly a quarter of the entire population of the globe. The inhabitants of all Britain's remaining Colonies now amount to not more than about 35 million, distributed among forty-three separately governed territories.

As the process nears its end, it is the most difficult cases that are left. There are territories with multi-racial populations such as Kenya and Rhodesia, which present special problems. There are those which are too small to bear the burdens of separate statehood, as for example St. Helena, with its population of only 5,000. There are also those, such as Hong Kong or the High Commission Territories of Basutoland, Bechuanaland and Swaziland, which do not, in present circumstances, wish to lose the protection of Britain. Since conditions vary from territory to territory, no standard formula can be universally applied.
Digitized by Google
The Modern Commonwealth
PART II
But, with patience and flexibility, and with the co-operation of the populations concerned, we shall, I am sure find fair and workable solutions to these different problems.

Britain has no desire to hold on to her remaining Colonies a day longer than is necessary. Politically, they involve us in much unwelcome controversy with the outside world; and economically we draw no profit from our sovereignty. We could, of course, pitchfork all our colonial territories into independence overnight, as some members of the United Nations are pressing us to do. But we consider we have a responsibility to the inhabitants, which we must fully discharge. We have a duty, not only to lead them to independence, but also to prepare them for the responsibilities which this involves.

While we are completing the final stage of this historic task, we ask for patience and understanding. On 4th October 1961, President Nkrumah said that he recognised 'the sincere determination of the British Government to find as quickly as possible a just and final solution of the colonial problem'; and he advised the peoples of the remaining British Colonies 'to rely on the declared intentions and good faith of the British Government and to press their case for early independence by constitutional means'. We hope that our other Commonwealth partners will believe likewise in our sincerity and will give us the encouragement of their trust and confidence.
THE OLD AND THE NEW
While we in Britain are working wholeheartedly to bring the new Commonwealth into being, we remain immensely proud of the achievements of the old British Empire.

When we travel through the newly independent countries of the Commonwealth, we are proud to see the railways, the harbours, the irrigation works, the industries, the democratic institutions, the systems of law and justice and countless other things which were created during the period of British administration. We are likewise proud of the part which Britain and her Empire played in preserving relative tranquillity throughout the world during the most of the nineteenth century—'Pax Britannica', as it has been called.

But if we are justly proud of the achievements of the old British Empire, we are equally proud of having converted it peacefully and amicably into the new independent Commonwealth—a development without parallel in history. We are a forward-looking people. We are not clinging nostalgically to the last vestiges of our imperial glory. On the contrary, the task of building a worldwide fellowship of free peoples and of forging links of understanding between them has inspired us with a new sense of mission.
SOVEREIGNTY AND WORLD ORDER

At a time when the speed of the jet aircraft is annihilating distance, when economic development is making all nations more and more dependent upon one another, when the nuclear bomb and the ballistic rocket have made war a global threat and peace a global responsibility, when the impelling need is to find world solutions to world problems, it may seem a little incongruous that we should be busy creating more and more separate sovereign states.
The desire for independent self-government is a natural and legitimate aspiration; but in our modern world the process of constitutional evolution cannot end there.
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
The Modern Commonwealth

If the achievement of national sovereignty were to be regarded as the final objective, then there would indeed be grounds for anxiety. For there can be no doubt that the true interests of all peoples will be achieved, not so much by asserting rights of sovereignty in support of separate national aims, as by pooling sovereignty and exercising it collectively for the common good of all. While each of the countries of the Commonwealth rightly prizes its individual national identity, I am sure that all of them, and not least those which have recently achieved independence, realise that ever closer international co-operation offers the only sure road to peace and progress.
'FAMILY' OR 'CLUB'?

The Commonwealth has been variously described as a family or a club. There is undoubtedly a family spirit which runs through the Commonwealth, though the members of the family are now grown up and living on their own. As in a family, there are feelings of affection and affinity between its members; and, as in most families, its members do not always see eye to eye.

The Commonwealth is also in a sense a club which, like all clubs, brings together people who have certain things in common. But membership of the Commonwealth club naturally does not prevent us from belonging to other clubs as well, where we come together with other people with whom we share other interests.

While it may be difficult to define just what the Commonwealth is, it is easy to explain some of the things it is not. Above all, it is not in any sense a pressure group. Unlike many international associations, it does not exist to promote or defend the sectional interests of its members.

Still less is the Commonwealth an alliance or an ideological bloc. Some Commonwealth countries like Britain, Canada, Pakistan, Australia and New Zealand are members of defensive alliances. Others like India, Ceylon, Nigeria and Ghana believe in non-alignment. Moreover, some belong to racial or geographical groups whose policies are at variance with those of other members. Quite often we vote on different sides at the United Nations; and sometimes we do things which deeply offend one another.
COMMON INSTITUTIONS

This diversity of interests and loyalties might be regarded as a source of weakness. But that would be to misunderstand completely the nature and spirit of our relationship. The potential strength of the Commonwealth derives precisely from the fact that the countries which compose it are so different, and that each of them has its own sphere of influence. As a Nigerian Minister said recently, 'the more individual members of the Commonwealth participate in the international affairs of their respective regions, the more will the collective influence of the Commonwealth as a whole be enlarged'.

With its extraordinarily varied membership, which embraces peoples of all races, creeds and continents, it is not very surprising that Commonwealth countries often hold different opinions on world problems. What is much more remarkable is that they all wish to maintain this special association with each other.

What is it that holds us together? What are the links that unite us? First there is the Queen. All our countries, whether they are monarchies or republics, recognise Her Majesty as the Head of the Commonwealth and the symbol of
Digitized by Google
The Modern Commonwealth
PART II

its unity. But apart from this formal role, Queen Elizabeth personally plays a most important part. Her frequent tours and the enthusiasm which they evoke are a very real factor in cementing the cohesion of the Commonwealth. As the Indian High Commissioner in London, Mrs. Pandit, said after the Royal tour of India, 'the vivid image of Queen Elizabeth will live in the memories of the multitudes who saw her pass, and the values she symbolises will help to bring us closer to one another'.

The Queen is not only the symbol of our present unity. She is also a reminder of the common past which we have shared together, in some cases for more than 200 years. As a result of this long association, we have become accustomed to think on much the same lines about most of the basic issues of life. We have developed a common belief in such things as the dignity of man, freedom of speech, equality before the law, the independence of the judiciary, honest and impartial administration, representative government and respect for the rights of minorities. To put these high principles fully into practice may not be easy in all the circumstances; but they provide a set of common standards, which all are honourably committed to try and achieve.

Our common political institutions are a particularly powerful bond between us. The Parliaments of most Commonwealth countries are conducted on very similar lines. There are of course differences, and this is only to be expected: for the political system cannot be divorced from the general background of the country concerned. We in Britain are fortunate in being a politically united, relatively prosperous and racially homogeneous society. Our Westminster system of parliamentary democracy was evolved over the years to suit those conditions; and it is remarkable how well it has proved its worth in circumstances so different from our own. Some variations may, however, be required in countries where an economic and social revolution is in progress or where there are deep racial, religious, or regional cleavages.
CONTINUOUS CONSULTATION

Undoubtedly the closest and most effective political link between our countries is the continuous contact which is maintained between Commonwealth Governments. Constant and informal consultation on current events is the very lifeblood of our special relationship.

Any issue which is of importance to one member of the Commonwealth cannot be a matter of indifference to the others. As British Commonwealth Secretary, I make it a practice to keep other Commonwealth Governments informed of all developments of importance with which Britain is concerned in the international field and to tell them our thoughts about the future, so that they may have the opportunity to give us their views and advice. For example, during the past year I have been in frequent communication with them about the negotiations over Laos, the control of nuclear tests, and the problems of the Congo and Berlin. Similarly, other Commonwealth Governments keep us constantly posted about these and other matters which are of special concern to them. This continuous exchange of views does not necessarily achieve an identity of policies, but it greatly helps us all to understand one another's problems and to work together for their solution.
The scale of this traffic of mutual information and consultation can be judged from the fact that, in addition to communications sent by post, the Common-
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
The Modern Commonwealth

wealth Relations Office in London despatches about 35,000 telegrams a year to our Missions in other Commonwealth countries. About two-thirds of these messages deal with foreign affairs and other political and economic matters.

These regular exchanges of information and opinions are conducted in an atmosphere of mutual confidence and frankness, which is rare in international relations. The climax of these consultations is the meeting of Commonwealth Prime Ministers, who sit and talk together privately and informally, almost as though they were colleagues in the same Cabinet. There are also annual meetings of Commonwealth Finance Ministers, the last of which was held in Accra. In addition, other Ministers and officials frequently come together to discuss specific problems. A recent example is the Commonwealth Education Conference in Delhi.
COMMONWEALTH MACHINERY

A basic feature of the Commonwealth is the absence of any formal constitution. Relations between members are not governed by any rules. There are no 'majority decisions'. At meetings of governments, conclusions are normally reached on the basis of what can best be described as the 'broad consensus of opinion', which naturally requires a reasonable measure of give and take on all sides. There are obviously issues on which it is not possible to achieve a common approach; but, when we have to disagree, we do so with regret and with an understanding of each other's points of view.

In this flexibility and mutual tolerance lie much of the Commonwealth's strength and power to endure. There are, however, those who think that, with its growing membership and varied composition, it will be obliged to abandon the present informality and intimacy of its proceedings. It has even been suggested that decisions should be reached by some process of voting. Apart from the fact that its independent member states, with their widely differing political and economic problems, could never accept the implications of this proposal, it would destroy the whole character of our relationship.

From time to time it has been suggested that a joint 'Commonwealth Secretariat' should be set up. It is argued that this would strengthen the links between us. But the proposal has so far been resisted, on the grounds that a new organisation of this kind would inevitably cut across the direct personal relations between ministers and officials in different Commonwealth countries, which is such a special feature of our association. On the other hand, Commonwealth Governments have found it useful to set up joint bodies to study a variety of common problems. Outstanding examples are the Commonwealth Economic Committee and the Commonwealth Agricultural Bureaux, which are financed by contributions from the member Governments. These organisations carry out continuous studies of Commonwealth trade and of technical progress in agriculture, and regularly publish reports which are highly valued by experts throughout the world. Machinery for practical co-operation in other fields is provided by the Commonwealth Telecommunications Board, the Commonwealth Education Liaison Committee, the Commonwealth Shipping Committee, the Commonwealth Aeronautical Research Council, the Commonwealth Scientific Committee and a variety of similar bodies in other fields.
ASSOCIATION OF PEOPLES
Important as are these manifold official relations, we must always remember that the Commonwealth is not just an association of governments. It is an
Digitized by Google
The Modern Commonwealth
PART II
association of peoples, between whom there are countless connections which need to be continuously refreshed and strengthened.

There is a vast network of personal and business contacts among those engaged in commerce. There are close links between professional men, such as doctors, lawyers and architects, who enjoy special facilities to practise in other Commonwealth countries. Valuable connections have grown up between us through art, sport and other activities, whilst the constant interchange of students helps to bring Commonwealth citizens of the rising generation into touch with one another. In addition there are the strong links forged by the Churches, whose missions have by their work done so much to develop mutual confidence and respect. There exist also numerous bodies which bring together from different Commonwealth countries people with similar interests and experience. A notable example is the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association, which at its annual conferences provides a forum for an independent exchange of views between members of Parliaments from all over the Commonwealth. There is also the Commonwealth Press Union, with its branches in member countries, and Commonwealth societies of many kinds.

All these existing relationships—official, commercial, professional and personal—are of immense value. But, having regard to the size and diversity of the Commonwealth and its peoples, this is not nearly enough. The contacts between us must be multiplied and greatly extended in every direction.

For historical reasons, there is a tendency for some people to regard the Commonwealth association as consisting primarily of the mutual relationship between Britain and the other member countries. Britain is proud to be the founder and centre of the Commonwealth. But if the Commonwealth is to attain its full stature, it is essential that the other members should develop closer and more direct links with each other.
ENGLISH LANGUAGE

In considering the factors which hold the Commonwealth together, we must not underrate the importance of the English language which is common to us all. English is not only the world's international language; it provides also a link between racial groups within the Commonwealth.

Those who wish to maintain and strengthen the Commonwealth should give special attention to the teaching of English. This matter was fully discussed at the recent Commonwealth Education Conference in Delhi. The need to increase the number of qualified teachers was strongly stressed; and it was decided to set up a Commonwealth English Languages Information Centre.
SENTIMENT

Finally, there is the bond of what is commonly called 'sentiment'—that feeling of instinctive affection which exists between members of a family. Commonwealth sentiment is an emotion; but it is not, for that reason, any the less important. As history shows, the emotions of peoples often exercise as much influence upon events as logic or self-interest.
Commonwealth sentiment is something precious, which all its citizens must guard and sustain. But we must be careful never to abuse or exploit it. Above all, we must never take it for granted.
In particular, we must not overlook the fact that the close friendship and understanding, which exists today between Britain and the new members of
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
The Modern Commonwealth

the Commonwealth, rests in no small measure on the personal good feelings which were established by a succession of British administrators, soldiers, and professional men during the colonial period. On the day of independence, Nigeria's Prime Minister, Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa, spoke of these men in generous and moving words which we shall long remember. He said that Nigeria had known them 'first as masters, then as leaders, finally as partners, but always as friends'.

But our future relationship cannot rest on past goodwill alone. The many personal links, which inevitably come to an end with independence, must be replaced by other personal links of a different kind. For, if the spirit of the Commonwealth is to endure as a living reality, and not as a dying ember of the past, it must in each new generation be refreshed and fostered by new contacts and the sharing of new experiences.
MUTUAL AID
We have examined what the Commonwealth is and what holds it together. Let us now consider what it can do to help solve the problems of the world.

One of the most pressing of these is poverty, which needs to be abolished, not only on grounds of social justice, but also because it constitutes a danger to peace. For so long as some nations live in affluence and others in penury, there is bound to be envy and mutual distrust, and those are the seeds of tension and conflict. Happily there has during recent years been a growing awareness that we all live in one world, and that prosperity, like peace, is indivisible. The industrially advanced nations now generally recognise that they have not only a moral duty, but also a practical interest in helping others.

The main difficulty arises from the fact that the populations of the poorer countries are many times more numerous than those of the rich. Therefore, even if it were practicable, any redistribution of wealth on the basis of 'equal shares' would bring about no very appreciable rise in the average standard of living. The objective must, therefore, be not to level down, but to level up.

The members of the Commonwealth have shown how fully they appreciate the importance and urgency of this problem. It was in this spirit that, ten years ago, they conceived the Colombo Plan, which was subsequently extended to embrace countries outside the Commonwealth. This great-hearted enterprise has succeeded in mobilising practical assistance of all kinds for the developing countries of Southern Asia. In addition, it has rightly established the principle that aid should be regarded, not as charity, but as mutual help between partners and friends.

Direct financial assistance in the form of loans or gifts is in many cases essential to developing countries. But it is, of course, the ultimate aim of every nation to make itself not only politically, but also economically independent. This process can be greatly accelerated if outside firms can be encouraged to start up enterprises in the newly developing countries. Naturally their readiness to do so will be largely determined by the extent to which they have confidence in the stability of the country concerned and in the security it offers to investors and traders.

In addition to commercial investment, we in Britain are now providing economic and technical aid from Government sources, at the rate of nearly £200 million a year; and we shall continue to play our part, up to the limit that our resources allow.
Digitized by Google
The Modern Commonwealth
PART II

But aid, like trade, is a two-way affair. If Britain is to make her full contribution to the economic life of the Commonwealth, her own economy must be in sound condition. That means that we must have a healthy balance of payments.

Before we in Britain can offer financial support to others, whether it be in the form of gifts or loans or capital investment, we have first to earn the money ourselves by selling the goods we produce in the markets of the world. Thus our ability to help develop the economies of our Commonwealth partners depends directly upon the development of our own trade overseas. We therefore look to them, in their own interests as well as ours, to do all they can to foster and encourage our trade with them.
COMMONWEALTH TRADE

Mutual trade between Britain and other members of the Commonwealth is one of the most important elements in the economic structure of all our countries. It is true that, after many years of continuous expansion, the trend has recently been in the other direction. But even so about 40 per cent. of Britain's imports come from other Commonwealth countries and about the same proportion of Britain's exports are bought by them. In every case, except that of Canada, Britain is the largest customer and also the largest supplier.
OVER ONE THIRD OF THE EXTERNAL TRADE OF ALL COMMONWEALTH COUNTRIES IS WITH ONE ANOTHER

The pattern of Britain's trade with other Commonwealth countries has for a long time been conducted on the principles of unrestricted free entry of most Commonwealth products into Britain and tariff preferences for Britain's exports. This system, which was consolidated in the Ottawa Agreements of 1932, has given the British consumer cheap food and raw materials and has encouraged other Commonwealth countries to look to Britain for the bulk of their requirements of imported manufactured goods. At the same time it has provided the farmers in the Commonwealth with an open market for their produce.

The situation has, however, been steadily changing. Since the thirties, there has been a marked revival of agriculture in Britain, which now produces a higher proportion of its own requirements. The result is that its capacity to absorb increased quantities of imported foodstuffs has not kept pace with the steadily mounting food production of Commonwealth countries. Parallel with this development in Britain, there has been a rapid growth of manufacturing industries in other Commonwealth countries and the raising of tariff barriers to protect them, with the result that many of the Commonwealth markets for Britain's traditional exports are becoming increasingly restricted. The developing economies of other Commonwealth countries have, however, opened up to British manufacturers new outlets for the sale of capital equipment and specialised goods of all kinds.
In order to take account of this changing pattern of Commonwealth trade, the Ottawa Agreements have in recent years had to be revised; and in due course further adjustments will doubtless be needed.
EUROPEAN COMMON MARKET
Britain is at present engaged in negotiations to join the European Common Market; and it is understandable that there should be anxiety in Common-
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
The Modern Commonwealth

wealth countries about the possible effects upon their trading interests. The extent of this anxiety varies from country to country according to the degree of its dependence upon the British market. For example, at one extreme, New Zealand sends to Britain over 90 per cent. of her basic exports, lamb and butter, and would be well-nigh ruined if this market were closed to her. At the other extreme, Malaya need have no fear for the sales of her tin and rubber which are already admitted free of duty into the Common Market.

We in Britain are as keen as anyone to maintain and expand Commonwealth trade, not only for its material value, but also for the part it plays in the life and unity of the Commonwealth. We are determined that Britain's entry into the Common Market should not be bought at the price of destroying the long established ties between us. We have made it clear that if we were faced with the necessity of choosing between the Commonwealth and Europe, we should unquestionably choose the Commonwealth.

But we do not believe our friends in Europe will confront us with that painful choice. For they have shown that they too fully recognise the value of the Commonwealth. As Monsieur Spaak said in the Belgian Parliament: 'No European can wish to see the destruction of the Commonwealth. A practical solution must be found which will enable Britain to join the Common Market and will at the same time safeguard her relationship with the Commonwealth'.

It should not, therefore, be assumed that, in drawing closer to Europe, Britain will be asked to draw away from the Commonwealth. What is more likely is that, through Britain's entry into the Common Market, Europe and the Commonwealth will tend to develop closer trade relations with one another; and that could only be to the advantage of both.

Of all organisations, the Commonwealth is the one which should least be afraid of change. It has never been a static association. Its ability to adapt itself continuously to new conditions is the whole secret of its successful evolution. The Commonwealth is itself the child of change.
RACE RELATIONS
Important as are the issues raised by the Common Market, we are faced by others of a much more fundamental character.

Every age has its own particular problems. The most complex and intractable problem of the second half of the twentieth century is undoubtedly that of race relations. As the growth of nationalism has spread from Europe to Asia and Africa, there has developed a heightened consciousness of race and colour. This has introduced new tensions and emotions, which make the dispassionate consideration of international issues extremely difficult.

Within the Commonwealth itself, this problem came to a head in an acute form over the issue of 'Apartheid'; and it was resolved only by the withdrawal of South Africa. That unmistakably dissociated the Commonwealth from South Africa's racial policies; but that is not enough. The Commonwealth, which is the greatest multi-racial association the world has ever seen, has a more positive part to play.

It has an historic opportunity to show by its example that peoples of different races can understand each other's viewpoints and respect each other's feelings; that they can discuss together objectively even the most explosive topics, with a view to finding reasonable solutions which all can support; and that they can work together in amity and confidence for the common good.
Digitized by Google
The Modern Commonwealth
PART II

This is the greatest challenge with which the Commonwealth has yet been faced. If it comes successfully through this test, it will have proved its worth to the world; and its ability to play a constructive part in other spheres will be immeasurably enhanced.
INFLUENCE FOR PEACE
The bigger and the more world-wide the problem, the better is the Commonwealth suited to help in its solution.

The human race is faced with many great problems; but the greatest of them all is war. If another war were to break out between major military powers, it would be impossible to restrict its area or to limit the types of weapons employed. The conflict would inevitably be global and nuclear. It would engulf everyone everywhere. The neutrals and the belligerents alike would suffer together.

Although the case for disarmament is so obvious and unanswerable and although all the people of the world passionately desire it, the progress of negotiations has so far been disappointing. In a situation of that kind, when deadlock seems complete, the Commonwealth has the opportunity to make its own special contribution.

It was in that spirit that the Commonwealth Prime Ministers, in March 1961, issued a joint appeal for total and universal disarmament, the simultaneous abolition of both conventional and nuclear weapons, and the creation of a world authority to exercise inspection and control. In a full statement, which did not seek to gloss over controversial issues, they set out a number of clear principles, which they suggested might provide a basis for reconciling Soviet and Western differences.

This joint declaration was presented to the United Nations and was also brought to the attention of the various international groups to which Commonwealth countries belong, including the conference of Non-Aligned States held at Belgrade in September 1961.

Apart from its own direct value, this joint initiative represents a significant new step in the evolution of the Commonwealth association. For it is the first occasion on which the members of the Commonwealth have sought, on a precise issue, to exercise a collective influence in international affairs. This practice, once started, will no doubt develop. Thus it may be expected that, from time to time, the governments of the Commonwealth will, by one means or another, endeavour to give a joint lead to the world on current problems.
SUPREME MISSION

In addition to the general influence which all nations in differing degree possess, every country of the Commonwealth has its own circle of friends among whom its voice is listened to with special attention. Each belongs to one, or in some cases more than one, political, economic or regional association, such as NATO, SEATO, EFTA, and the Afro-Asian Group. Through these wide-ranging and overlapping connections, Commonwealth governments are in intimate touch with the thoughts and intentions of most of the nations of the world and are able to make known their views to them.

However, it is obvious that, if the Commonwealth is to exercise a collective influence among other nations, its members must themselves be agreed on the issue in question. That is by no means easy. In fact, having regard to their differing outlooks and often conflicting policies, it may well be asked: 'How can the Commonwealth hope to play any effective part in world affairs?'
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 17
The Modern Commonwealth

The role which the Commonwealth can perform is determined by the character of its own membership—nations of all races, spread over all continents, some of them members of defence alliances, others neutralist and unaligned, some highly industrialised, others underdeveloped. Together they reflect in miniature almost every facet of the world scene. Such a group of nations could never unite for aggression; nor could they 'gang up' together to pursue selfish ends. The economic needs and political backgrounds of its peoples are so varied that the Commonwealth could never constitute a threat or a danger to others.

Its influence cannot be other than pacific and constructive. Its role is to work for the realisation of the basic aspirations of humanity—peace, freedom and the rule of law. No task is today more important or more urgent, and no group of nations is more naturally fitted to discharge it.

At this time, when the world is torn with suspicion and envy and the whole human race is in danger of destroying itself, we in the Commonwealth have a momentous opportunity to exercise a healing and unifying influence. Through its world-wide character and the intimate ties which unite it, the Commonwealth is in a unique position to help bridge the differences between nations and races and build up confidence among them.

But before we set out to promote understanding among others, we must first establish it more firmly among ourselves. Above all, we must show that we are able to see things from each other's point of view and are ready at all times to put the most generous interpretation on one another's words and actions. Instead of highlighting the things which divide us, we must show ourselves capable of working constructively together for the ideals which are common to us all.

If we succeed in creating a true sense of trust and co-operation among the varied members of the Commonwealth, the inspiration of our example might well prove a decisive factor in the solution of the wider problems which divide and threaten mankind.
To show the way—that is the supreme mission of our modern Commonwealth.

CHAPTER 18
CANADA
GOVERNOR-GENERAL AND COMMANDER-IN-CHIEF His Excellency Major-General Georges P. Vanier, DSO, MC, CD
GENERAL

CANADA occupies the northern half of the North American continent stretching from latitude $49^\circ$ to beyond the polar circle, but does not include Alaska, which is part of the United States, or the small islands of St. Pierre and Miquelon, off the coast of Newfoundland, which belong to France. North of the mainland Canada includes the Canadian Arctic Archipelago and, still further north across the Viscount Melville Sound, the Queen Elizabeth Islands. The main islands of the former group are Baffin, Victoria, Banks, Prince of Wales and Somerset Islands, and of the latter group Ellesmere, Devon, Melville, Axel Heiberg, Prince Patrick and Bathurst Islands. Other islands of importance are Vancouver Island and the Queen Charlotte Islands off the Pacific coast; Southampton Island in Hudson Bay; Cape Breton, Anticosti and Prince Edward Islands in the Gulf of St. Lawrence; and Newfoundland. Canada is the second largest country in the world comprising an area computed at 3,851,809 square miles of land and fresh water, over forty times the area of Britain.

The Canadian federal state was established by the British North America Act, 1867, and now consists of ten Provinces and two Territories. The Provinces with the dates on which they joined the Confederation, are Ontario (1867), Quebec (1867), Nova Scotia (1867), New Brunswick (1867), Manitoba (1870), British Columbia (1871), Prince Edward Island (1873), Saskatchewan (1873), Alberta (1905) and Newfoundland (1949). The Territories are the Northwest Territories (1870) and the Yukon Territory (1895). The Northwest Territories were divided in 1920 into the Districts of Mackenzie, Keewatin and Franklin.

According to the 1961 census the population of the whole of Canada on 1st June 1961 was 18,238,247. At the first five-year census of Canada in 1956 the population was 16,080,791. At the 1951 census the population was 14,009,429 of whom 4,319,167 were of French extraction, mostly residing in Quebec where French is generally spoken. The 1951 census included 11,500 Eskimos scattered throughout the northern mainland and the Arctic islands. The main groups live along the northern mainland coast near the mouth of the Mackenzie River; on Baffin Island; in northern Quebec and on the east shore of Hudson Bay. There were also 32,528 persons of Chinese and 21,663 of Japanese origin living in Canada. The population of indigenous Indian origin numbered 155,874. According to the departmental census 1960, the Indian population at 31st December 1960 totalled 185,169. With the exception of some nomadic groups in the outlying northern regions the Indians live in approximately six hundred communities
For further information about Canada see Canada Year Book .
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 18
Canada

known as 'bands'. The bands are situated in over 2,200 Reserves, located across Canada in the mainland provinces and the two northern territories. The Indians are not a single race but are divided into ten basic linguistic groups. Six of these, Tsimshian, Salisham, Kootenayan, Wakashan, Haida and Tlinkit, are located in British Columbia; four of them, Algonkian, Athapaskan, Iroquian and Siouan are found east of the Rocky Mountains. These basic groups are subdivided into tribal groups with many local dialects and differing cultural characteristics. The Indians of the Algonkian stock are the most numerous, extending from the Atlantic Ocean to the Rocky Mountains. The Iroquian stock is found mainly in Ontario and Quebec. The Athapaskan stock inhabit the Northwest Territories and the Yukon while tribes of the Sioux inhabit certain regions in Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta.

Under the provisions of the British North America Act, 1867, either the English or the French language may be used in debates in the Parliament of Canada and in the Legislature of Quebec and either of these languages may be used by any person or in any Pleading or Process in or issuing from any Court of Canada established under the Act and in or from all or any of the Courts of Quebec. Results of the 1961 census showed that 12,284,762 persons spoke English, 3,489,866 spoke French only, 2,231,172 were bilingual whilst 232,447 spoke neither English nor French.

The principal international airports, with distances in miles from town centres are: Edmonton (3); Gander; Montreal (Dorval) (14); Ottawa (Uplands) (8); Toronto (Malton) (18); Winnipeg (4); and Vancouver (9).
The principal seaports (tonnages in millions) are: Vancouver (18.86); Montreal (11.99); Nanaimo, B.C. (6.85); Victoria (6.77); Halifax (6.18); Quebec (5.59) and Port Arthur, Ont. (4.45).

The total selling value of all Canadian industrial factory shipments for 1960 amounted to $23,747.5 million. The total for the forty leading industries amounted to $16,342.3 million. The four principal industries were: Pulp and Paper $1,578.7 m.; Non-ferrous metal smelting and refinery $1,495.2 m.; Petroleum products $1,148.9 m. and Slaughtering and Meat-packing $1,058.4 m.

Principal domestic exports for the calendar year 1961 were: Newsprint paper $761.3 m.; Wheat $661.8 m.; Lumber and Timber $354.9 m.; Wood pulp $346.7 m.; Nickel and Products $338.8 m.; Aluminium (primary and semi-fabricated) $250.7 m.; Copper (ditto) $201.8 m.; Uranium ores and concentrates $192.7 m.; Iron ore $142.6 m.; Asbestos $131.3 m.

The National Day of Canada is 1st July, Dominion Day.
THE NATIONAL CAPITAL

Ottawa, designated the National Capital of Canada at Confederation in 1867, is situated in an area of great natural beauty at the junction of the Ottawa, Rideau and Gatineau rivers. Although the city remains a self-governing municipality, the Federal Government is carrying out through the medium of the National Capital Commission a Master Plan (approved in 1946), designed to guide the development of the Capital's urban area over the following fifty years and to protect and develop the natural beauty of the National Capital District of about 1,800 square miles. The major projects under the Master Plan are concerned with the development of open space, parkways, the relocation of railways, the decentralization of new government building construction, the provision of a green belt and the development of Gatineau Park in the Laurentian Hills to the North.
HISTORY

The History of Canada—a transcontinental federal state today embracing ten Provinces and two vast northern Territories in a union second in size among the nations to that of its Soviet Russian neighbour across the Arctic polar region—extends back over four centuries to the explorations of John Cabot (1497) in the service of England and of Jacques Cartier in the service of France (1534), two navigators who symbolize the early colonial rivalry of Britain and France in the New World climaxed in the fall of Quebec (1759) and the Treaty of Paris (1763), ceding Canada to Great Britain.

History and geography have combined during the two subsequent centuries to produce a bicultural nation with a story of heroic achievement and an enriched cultural heritage. Its geographical framework had been roughly staked out as fur-trading areas by French-Canadian explorers and voyageurs and by the Hudson's Bay and North-West Companies (united 1821). These intrepid traders, lords of Canada's unmatched lakes and rivers and its immense plains and forests, traversed and controlled the great expanses of territory linking the five diverse and widely separated areas of British territory that survived the American War of Independence (1775–1783) and awaited a set of circumstances that goaded their struggling populations to seek political and economic survival almost 100 years ago in a federal system of government. Canada's story since 1867 is one of acquisition of Rupert's Land and the North-Western Territory (1870) and the Arctic Archipelago (1880) from Great Britain, and further settlement and development
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 18
Canada

of diverse regions—the Atlantic provinces, the distinctively French-Canadian St. Lawrence Valley, the old Loyalist Upper Canada province of Ontario, the vast expanse of the western prairies, and the then remote Pacific coast province of British Columbia.

It is a romantic story of transcontinental railway building, of opening the prairie West to an early twentieth-century tide of settlement which did much to stimulate the industrial development of Ontario and British Columbia and provide one of the principal sources of national pride; a story of the attainment of industrial maturity and a predominantly manufacturing economy through the marked impetus two world wars gave to the discovery of new sources of power and of metallic minerals and mineral fuels and to the forced application of a whole new series of innovations in industrial technology; a story of the emergence in recent decades of a strong new sense of Canadian national identity and consciousness, a new concept of democracy based on ethnic and cultural diversity or the doctrine of 'unity in diversity'. No less fundamental to this growth of national consciousness has been Canada's evolution during the past half century to complete sovereign status in domestic and international affairs—a peaceful and piecemeal evolution of the foundations of nationhood from colonialism through self-governing dominionhood to the status of leader among the Middle Powers without destroying old associations. And in this unique fashion of nation-building Canada's influence and experience have been major elements in the formation of the British Commonwealth of Nations. Even in the larger post-war international community, Canada's role has been marked by moderation, impartiality and devotion to the ideals of peace and freedom when confronting the grave issues of the modern world.
CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT
The territorial and constitutional development of the transcontinental federation of Canada is set out in the succeeding paragraphs.

Prior to the British North America Act of 1867, British America consisted of the Colonies of Canada (the provinces of Ontario and Quebec), New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland in the east and the Colony of British Columbia in the west, the vast central and northern territory being known as Rupert's Land (the territory which drained into the Hudson Bay) and the North-Western Territory. There were also lands to the north which were virtually unexplored.

In 1867 the British North America Act grouped the Colonies of Canada, New Brunswick and Nova Scotia into a confederation, to which was given the name of Canada. This Act was brought into force on 1st July 1867 by Royal Proclamation dated 22nd May 1867.

By Order in Council dated 23rd June 1870, following the introduction of the Rupert's Land Act, 1868, Rupert's Land and the North-Western Territory were transferred to Canada with effect from 15th July 1870. The combined territories were designated as The North-West Territories.

On the date of the transfer a part of the North-West Territories, by the Manitoba Act, 1870, was formed into a new province called the Province of Manitoba (its boundaries being later extended in 1881). On the same day the new Province was admitted separately into the Union of Canada. A Lieutenant-Governor was appointed to govern Manitoba, and by a separate commission
Digitized by Google
Canada
PART III
the Governor of Manitoba was appointed as the Lieutenant-Governor of the North-West Territories.
By Order in Council dated 16th May 1871 the Colony of British Columbia was admitted into the confederation on 20th July 1871.
Prince Edward Island was admitted by Order in Council of 26th June 1873 on the 1st July of that year.

On 31st July 1880, in compliance with the prayer of an Address from the Parliament of Canada dated 3rd May 1878, Her Majesty issued an Order in Council annexing to Canada from 1st September 1880 all British Territories in North America not already included within Canada and all islands adjacent thereto, with the exception of the Colony of Newfoundland and its dependencies. These additional territories were formally included in the North-West Territories.

The Keewatin Act, 1876, provided for the formation of a separate district of the North-West Territories, to be known as the District of Keewatin, to the north of Manitoba. By Order in Council of 8th May 1882 the southern part of the North-West Territories was divided into the provisional Districts of Assiniboia, Saskatchewan, Alberta and Athabasca and by Order in Council of 2nd June 1895 further provisional Districts of Ungava, Franklin, Mackenzie and Yukon were created in the north of the Territories, the boundaries being redefined by Order in Council of 18th December 1897. Yukon was created a separate territory, distinct from the North-West Territories, by the Yukon Territory Act, 1898.

On 1st September 1905, by the Alberta Act, 1905, and the Saskatchewan Act, 1905, the Provinces of Alberta and Saskatchewan were formed from the provisional Districts of Alberta, Assiniboia, Saskatchewan and Athabasca, the dividing line running north and south.
The remainder of the North-West Territories were re-designated the Northwest Territories in 1906.

By a Federal Act of 1912 the boundaries of the Provinces of Ontario, Quebec and Manitoba were extended, the whole of Ungava being transferred to Quebec and parts of Keewatin to Ontario and Manitoba. The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council, by the Labrador Boundary Award made on 1st March 1927, adjudged that an area of about 112,000 square miles, ownership of which had been disputed between Quebec and Newfoundland, was part of the latter.

In 1949 Newfoundland, including Labrador, was joined by the British North America Act, 1949, with the existing nine Provinces as a tenth Province after the people of Newfoundland had by a majority voted in favour in a referendum held in 1948.
CONSTITUTION

The Executive Government is vested in the Crown and is exercised by a Governor-General appointed by the Queen on the recommendation of Her Majesty's Canadian Ministers. The Governor-General exercises his executive powers on the advice of his Cabinet which, as in England, is a committee of the Privy Council, formed of the principal members of the Government and responsible to the Parliament of Canada.

The supreme legislative power in the field of jurisdiction assigned to the federal legislature by the British North America Act is vested in a Parliament, consisting of the Queen (represented by the Governor-General), a Senate and a House of
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 18
Canada

Commons. The Senate now consists of 102 members nominated for life by the Governor-General and so chosen that 24 represent Ontario, 24 Quebec, 24 the Maritime Provinces, 24 the Western Provinces and (since 31st March 1949) 6 Newfoundland. The qualifications for Senator include the possession of property worth $4,000, age not less than 30 years and residence within the province for which he is appointed. The House of Commons consisted originally of 181 elected members. This number has been increased by additions on the accession of new provinces and as the result of increase in population. Representation in the House is reviewed decennially, and under an Act passed in 1952 the number of Members was raised to 265, distributed as follows: Ontario 85, Quebec 75, Nova Scotia 12, New Brunswick 10, Manitoba 14, British Columbia 22, Prince Edward Island 4, Saskatchewan 17, Alberta 17, Newfoundland 7, Yukon Territory 1 and Mackenzie District 1. There is no property qualification; the age qualification is 21 years. A Parliament lasts five years if not sooner dissolved.

For each province there is a Lieutenant-Governor, appointed by the Governor-General in Council and holding office during pleasure, but not removable within five years of appointment except for cause assigned.

Each province has a 'Legislative Assembly', and in Quebec there is also a 'Legislative Council', forming a second chamber. The Provincial Legislatures possess the power of altering their own constitutions. The territory not comprised within any province (Yukon and the Northwest Territories) is very thinly inhabited. The Yukon Territory is governed by an appointed Commissioner (under instructions from the Governor-General in Council or the Minister of Northern Affairs and National Resources) and an elective legislative council of seven members. The Northwest Territories are similarly governed by a Commissioner and nine councillors, of whom four are elected and the rest appointed by the Governor-General in Council.

The Parliament of Canada has exclusive legislative power in certain specified matters and generally in all matters except those specifically assigned by the constitution to the Provincial Legislatures. The Canadian Constitution is in this respect the reverse of that of the United States. Among matters enumerated by the British North America Act as lying within the federal sphere are: public finance, trade regulations, postal service, currency, coinage, banking, navigation, defence, law relating to crimes, bankruptcy, copyright, patents, naturalization and Indian affairs.

The Provincial Legislatures have powers to legislate in respect of certain specified subjects, of which the chief are property and civil rights, the alteration of their own constitutions, direct taxation within the province and provincial loans, the management of provincial public lands, provincial and municipal offices, hospitals, gaols, licences, local works, and the general civil law and procedure. Over education they have full powers, subject only to certain provisions to secure protection to religious minorities. In agricultural, quarantine and immigration matters they possess concurrent legislative powers with the Parliament of Canada.
The Judges are appointed by the Governor-General in the superior, district and county courts throughout Canada, except in the probate courts of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick.
By the provisions of the British North America Act and subsequent arrangements entered into from time to time, the Canadian Government is required to
Digitized by Google
Canada
PART III
make certain annual payments to the individual Provinces. These payments (subsidies) are of four kinds:

(a) Interest on Debt Allowances. This is based on payment of interest at 5 per cent per annum on the amount by which the actual debts of the Provinces on their entering into Confederation fall short of a prescribed debt allowance.
(b) Allowances for Government and Legislature. These are fixed amounts based on the population of the Province concerned and range from $100,000 to $240,000.

(c) Allowances per Head of Population. Grants are paid at the rate of 80 cents a head up to a population of $2\frac{1}{2}$ million, and at 60 cents a head for so much of the population as exceeds that number.
(d) Special Grants. A number of special grants have been sanctioned for various reasons.
The total paid to the Provinces under these statutory subsidies amounted to $23.5 million during the year ended 31st March 1962. Full details are given in the Public Accounts of Canada.

In addition the Provinces received $502.7 million under the Tax-Sharing Arrangements 1956 Act, $6.4 million by transfer of certain public utilities tax receipts, and $8.0 million additional grants to Newfoundland.

The British North America Act, 1867, provided that Bills passed by the Provincial Legislatures require the assent of the Lieutenant-Governor and may be disallowed within a year by the Governor-General, and those passed by the Federal Parliament require the assent of the Governor-General and may be disallowed within two years by the Queen.

Provision was made in the Act constituting Canada for the admission into it of any of the other British possessions on the continent of North America, and they have all since been admitted. Provision was also made for the construction of a railway from the River St. Lawrence to Halifax and (in the Order in Council admitting British Columbia) for the construction of a railway from the Atlantic to the Pacific Ocean. Both these have been constructed.

THE GOVERNMENT

The party standing following the General Election of 10th June 1957 ensured that the national leader of the Progressive Conservative Party and his colleagues would constitute the Ministry of the day, and that Ministry, under the leadership of Prime Minister John G. Diefenbaker, assumed office on 21st June 1957, thereby ending a 22-year period of Liberal administration. The 23rd Parliament, one of the shortest in the history of Canada, was dissolved on 1st February 1958, and the General Election of 31st March 1958 returned the Progressive Conservative Party to power with an overwhelming majority (208 Progressive Conservatives, 49 Liberals and 8 Co-operative Commonwealth Federation). The 24th Parliament was dissolved on 19th April 1962, and the General Election of 18th June 1962 did not provide any political party with an over-all majority of the seats. The party standing (on 25th June 1962) was as follows: 116 Progressive Conservatives, 99 Liberals, 30 Social Credit, 19 New Democratic Party (formerly C.C.F.) and one constituency election deferred. Mr. Diefenbaker therefore remained Prime Minister as leader of the largest party.

The total vote recorded at the June 1962 elections was 7,686,219, representing about 78.5 per cent of the eligible voters. The Conservatives obtained 37.31 per cent, Liberals 37.36 per cent, New Democratic Party 13.48 per cent and Social Credit 11.66 per cent.

OLD CANADA (QUEBEC AND ONTARIO)
HISTORY

The Atlantic coast was discovered by Leif Ericson about 1000 A.D. and re-discovered by John Cabot in 1497. Jacques Cartier landed at Gaspé in 1534 and sailed on to discover the St. Lawrence River. From 1603 onwards Samuel de Champlain explored the Atlantic seaboard, founding Port Royal (Nova Scotia) and Quebec (1608), and finally pushed up the Ottawa River to the upper Great Lakes. French explorers subsequently travelled the continent from Hudson Bay to the mouth of the Mississippi and from the Saguenay River to the Rockies.

Until 1663 Quebec was governed autocratically by a trading company; in that year it became a Royal Province, under a Governor to whom as the personal representative of the King of France were entrusted the general policy of the country, the direction of its military affairs and its relations with the Indian tribes. The Bishop, as head of the Church, was supreme in matters affecting religion, and the Intendant, acting under the authority of the King, not of the Governor, was responsible for the administration of justice, for finance, and for the direction of local administration. A Superior Council also existed with certain administrative powers more formal than real. This system continued until the end of the French regime.
In 1759 Quebec succumbed to British forces under General Wolfe, and in 1763 the whole territory of Canada was ceded to Britain by the Treaty of Paris.

From 1763 to 1774 Canada was governed in the main by military authority. In the latter year the Quebec Act, passed by the British Parliament, secured for the French colonists the right to retain their language, religion and civil law. Roman Catholics were allowed the free exercise of their religion and the Catholic clergy were declared entitled to receive their accustomed dues and rights with
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 18
Canada

respect to those professing the Catholic religion; and in civil cases it was declared that resort should be had to the Laws of Canada (i.e., the old French civil laws) for the decision. A nominee Council was also established. The boundaries were extended to the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers, but were withdrawn to the Great Lakes in 1783.

In 1791 an Act of Parliament was passed (31 Geo. III, Cap. 31) authorizing the Crown to issue a proclamation dividing Canada into two Provinces, Upper Canada (now Ontario), in which many loyalists from the American colonies had settled, and Lower Canada (now Quebec), and establishing a constitution for each. The first step towards democratic institutions was taken by the establishment of elected Houses of Assembly, though the Governors, appointed by the Crown, and the nominated legislative councils retained control. This resulted in increasing difficulties, especially in Lower Canada, between the Houses of Assembly and the Executive Governments, particularly after the removal of immediate danger to the Province with the end of the American War in 1814. These difficulties continued until the risings of 1837-38, which resulted in the suspension of the constitution of Lower Canada and the appointment of Lord Durham as Governor-General with special powers and instructions to report on the affairs of the country. He recommended effective control of the executive by the elected representatives of the people, i.e., responsible as distinct from representative government, and to this end the immediate union of Upper and Lower Canada and the ultimate union of all British North America. Upper and Lower Canada were accordingly reunited by Act 3 and 4 Vict. Cap. 35, and the legislative bodies were consolidated. The new House of Assembly, created in 1840, consisted of 84 members, there being an equal number from each Province. The new Legislative Council was to consist of not less than 20 members (it subsequently exceeded double that number), and the members were appointed for life. Subsequently (by 17 and 18 Vict. Cap. 118) the Canadian Legislature was empowered to alter the Constitution of the Legislative Council, and this power was utilized in 1856 by an Act of Canada which made half of the Legislative Council elective for terms of eight years. The modification was an important one, for it was because of the dissatisfaction caused by the behaviour of certain elected Councillors that, on the creation of the Dominion of Canada in 1867, the new Senate was made appointive.

As years passed, increasing difficulty was experienced in the conduct of public affairs owing to the division of interest of Upper and Lower Canada, while the development of communications and the outbreak of the Civil War in the United States emphasized both the possibility and the desirability of confederation. Negotiations resulted in agreement between Canada, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick and the passage of the British North America Act, 1867.
ONTARIO
SITUATION AND POPULATION

The Province stretches 1,000 miles from east to west, from Quebec to the Prairies, and 1,050 miles from south to north, from the Great Lakes to Hudson Bay. Its area is 412,582 square miles, and the population in 1961 was 6,236,092, about one-third of the total population of Canada. The capital is Toronto, the population of which was 672,407 in 1961, metropolitan Toronto having a population of 1,824,481. The population of the Province at 1st April 1962 was estimated at 6,321,000.

QUEBEC
SITUATION AND POPULATION

Quebec lies on both sides of the Lower St. Lawrence and extends from the New England states of the U.S.A. to the Davis Straits. Its area is 594,860 square miles. The population in 1961 was 5,259,211, of whom 2,109,509 live in greater Montreal, the world's largest inland port, which is up the St. Lawrence River 1,000 miles from the Atlantic Ocean. The capital city is Quebec, which has a population of 171,979. The population of greater Quebec is 357,568.
CONSTITUTION
(For development prior to 1867 see under 'Old Canada.')

The Legislature consists of the Legislative Council and the Legislative Assembly. The Legislative Council consists of 24 members, who are appointed for life by the Lieutenant-Governor in Council. The Legislative Assembly is elected for five years by general suffrage, with no property qualification. It consists of 95 members. The last election was held in 1960.
THE GOVERNMENT

NOVA SCOTIA
SITUATION AND POPULATION

Nova Scotia consists of the peninsula of Nova Scotia and the island of Cape Breton, both lying between the Gulf of St. Lawrence and the Atlantic Ocean. The area is 21,425 square miles. The population in 1961 was 737,007, of whom 92,511 live in the capital city, Halifax.
HISTORY AND CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT

Nova Scotia was first discovered by the Norsemen and rediscovered by John Cabot in 1497; it was colonized by the French in 1598; was taken by the English, and a grant of it made to Sir W. Alexander by James I in 1621. In 1632 it was restored to France, with Quebec, by the Treaty of St. Germain-en-Laye, but again ceded to England at the Peace of Utrecht in 1713. After the Peace of Aix-la-Chapelle in 1748, a settlement for disbanded troops was formed there by Lord Halifax, and the city which now bears his name is the capital of the province. Cape Breton was not finally taken from the French until 1758, in which year the
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 18
Canada

first Assembly was summoned. Some 35,000 loyalists moved to Nova Scotia from the former American colonies to the south when the independence of the latter was recognized in 1783 and the last British troops withdrawn. In 1769 Prince Edward Island became a separate colony and in 1784 New Brunswick and Cape Breton Island were also separated from the rest of Nova Scotia to which Cape Breton Island was later reunited. In 1848 responsible government was established, and in 1867 Nova Scotia was one of the four colonies which united to form Canada, of which it became a Province.
CONSTITUTION
The legislature now consists of a single House, the House of Assembly, of 43 members, elected for five years.
THE GOVERNMENT

The present Government is formed from the Progressive Conservative Party which secured 27 seats at the election in 1960. The Liberal Party secured 15 seats and the Co-operative Commonwealth Federation Party 1.

SITUATION AND POPULATION

New Brunswick is one of the Maritime Provinces of Canada and consists of the mainland between Quebec and Nova Scotia. The area of the Province is 28,354 square miles, and the population estimated at 1st June 1961 was 597,936. The provincial capital is Fredericton, with a population, estimated in 1961, of 19,683.
HISTORY AND CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT
New Brunswick was part of the ancient French Province of Acadia and was ceded to England by the Treaty of Utrecht in 1713. Great Britain, however, did
Digitized by Google

not obtain full possession of the country until after the fall of Quebec in 1759. It was first colonized by British subjects from New England in 1761, and in 1783, at the close of the Revolutionary War, it received a large body of Loyalists from the Thirteen Colonies. In 1784 it was separated from Nova Scotia, of which it had formed a part, and given a separate Governor and Assembly. The colony remained quiet and prosperous, largely free from the conflicts between Executive and Legislature which vexed the other North American colonies until 1837. In 1854 responsible government was established, and in 1867 New Brunswick was one of the colonies which agreed to form the Dominion of Canada, of which it became an original Province.

BRITISH COLUMBIA
SITUATION AND POPULATION

British Columbia is the westernmost Province in Canada and lies between the Rocky Mountains and the Pacific Ocean. The area (including Vancouver Island and Queen Charlotte Islands) is 366,255 square miles. The population in 1961 was 1,629,082, of whom 790,165 lived in the metropolitan Vancouver area and 154,152 in metropolitan Victoria, the capital city. HISTORY

British Columbia is an amalgamation of four Colonial jurisdictions. Vancouver Island was granted to the Hudson's Bay Company by Royal Charter in 1849, at which time the Crown Colony was established. In 1852 the Queen Charlotte Islands were established as a Lieutenant-Dependency of Vancouver Island. In consequence of a large migration on the discovery of gold on the Fraser and Thompson Rivers in 1858, the mainland Crown Colony of British Columbia was constituted, comprising roughly the southern half of the mainland. In 1862, the northern half of the mainland, including part of the present Yukon Territory was established as the Territory of Stikine. In 1863, the Queen Charlotte Islands, British Columbia and the Stikine Territory were united under the name of British Columbia. In 1866 this colony of British Columbia and Vancouver Island were united under the former name and in 1871 British Columbia became a province of Canada.
CONSTITUTION
The Legislature consists of a single House. The Legislature Assembly of 52 Members is elected for a maximum of five years.
THE GOVERNMENT

HISTORY AND CONSTITUTIONAL DEVELOPMENT

Prince Edward Island shared in the influx of Loyalists from the American colonies during and after the revolutionary war and was in consequence separated from Nova Scotia in 1769. The problem of absentee proprietors bedevilled the relations of Governor and Assembly for the next 60 years, but responsible government was established in 1851. The colony was not one of the original provinces of Canada but joined the Federation in 1873.
